title: Beyond Dark Mode: Building a 3-Theme System with CSS Variables and JavaScript
excerpt: Learn how to implement light, dark, and custom brand themes using modern CSS, a segmented toggle switch UI, and a few lines of JavaScript.

children.text: Dark mode has become standard across modern interfaces. But users today expect more than just light and dark — they expect choice and personalization.
In this tutorial, we’ll build a scalable 3-theme system with:
And instead of basic buttons, we’ll use a modern segmented toggle switch UI that feels polished and intuitive.
Start by defining reusable CSS variables for your UI:
Use a data-theme attribute on the <html> element to switch themes.
Step 3: Build a modern segmented toggle switch
Instead of separate buttons, we’ll build a sliding toggle control.
}/* Move slider based on active theme */
This creates a smooth animated toggle that slides between the three options.
Step 4: Add JavaScript to power the toggle
Now we connect the UI to your theme system.
keeps the UI toggle in sync
You now have a modern theming system with:
⚡ Instant UI updates via CSS variables
All in just a few lines of code.
Dark mode is just the starting point.

By combining CSS variables, a data attribute theme system, and a modern toggle switch, you can create a flexible theming architecture that grows with your product and gives users full control over their experience.
And the best part? It’s simple, fast, and completely framework-free.

Modern web development demands speed, scalability, and excellent user experiences. That's where Next.js comes in. Built on top of React, Next.js has become one of the most popular frameworks for creating fast, SEO-friendly, and production-ready web applications.
With the release of Next.js 16, developers gain access to improved performance, enhanced developer experience, and more streamlined workflows for building modern websites and applications.
In this guide, we'll explore what Next.js 16 is, why developers love it, and how you can get started with your first project.
Next.js is an open-source React framework developed by Vercel. It extends React by providing features that are commonly needed in production applications, such as:
Instead of spending time configuring complex tooling, Next.js allows developers to focus on building features.
Next.js 16 continues to improve performance and developer productivity. Some notable improvements include:
The framework includes improvements to development tooling, making local development faster and more responsive.
React Server Components continue to play a major role, allowing developers to reduce JavaScript sent to the browser and improve page performance.
Data fetching patterns are more predictable and efficient, helping applications load content faster while reducing unnecessary requests.
Next.js automatically optimizes rendering, images, fonts, and assets to deliver faster websites with minimal configuration.
Before creating a Next.js application, make sure you have:
Basic knowledge of JavaScript and React
You can verify your installation by running:
The easiest way to start is with the official project generator.
You'll be prompted with several setup options:
✔ Would you like to use TypeScript?
✔ Would you like to use ESLint?
✔ Would you like to use Tailwind CSS?
✔ Would you like to use the App Router?
For most projects, the default recommendations are a great choice.
Open your browser and navigate to:
You should see the default Next.js welcome page.
Next.js 16 uses the App Router by default.
A typical project structure looks like this:
No manual route configuration is required.
When saved, Next.js automatically updates the browser using Fast Refresh.
One of Next.js's strengths is server-side data fetching.
Because this runs on the server, users receive pre-rendered content, improving performance and SEO.
Dynamic routes allow you to create pages based on data.
will automatically be handled by this route.
Next.js includes a built-in Image component.
Search engine optimization is one of Next.js's strongest features.
You can define metadata directly within pages:
This helps search engines understand your content and improves visibility.
You can deploy to platforms such as:
Deployment is typically straightforward and requires minimal configuration.
Next.js 16 is an excellent choice for:
Its combination of performance, scalability, SEO capabilities, and developer-friendly tooling makes it one of the best frameworks available for modern web development.

Next.js 16 continues to push the boundaries of modern web development by combining the flexibility of React with powerful built-in features. Whether you're building a personal blog, a business website, or a large-scale application, Next.js provides the tools needed to create fast, scalable, and SEO-friendly experiences.
If you're already familiar with React, learning Next.js is a natural next step—and with version 16, getting started has never been easier.

title: Build a Fully Responsive Navigation Menu with Pure CSS (No JavaScript)
excerpt: This menu includes desktop dropdowns, mobile sidebar navigation, accordion submenus, overlay close behavior, and an animated hamburger icon.

children.text: In this tutorial, we will build a modern, fully responsive navigation menu using **pure CSS only** — no JavaScript required.
This menu includes desktop dropdowns, mobile sidebar navigation, accordion submenus, overlay close behavior, and an animated hamburger icon.
This menu uses the **CSS checkbox hack** to toggle the menu state, media queries for responsiveness, and `max-height` transitions for smooth accordion animations.

contentBlocks.code:
• MyApp
  • Home
    • Web Design
    • SEO
    • Marketing
    • Team
    • Mission
  • Contact

children.text: This approach is perfect for blogs, portfolios, and lightweight websites where you want a modern responsive menu without JavaScript.
You can customize colors, spacing, and menu items easily to match your brand.
